worth_it_explanation: An ads.txt file tells buyers which companies are authorized to sell a publisher's inventory, and a Prebid.js wrapper calls demand partners that each need a matching line. When a partner the wrapper calls has no ads.txt entry, or the wrong seller account id, buyers can treat that supply path as unauthorized and decline to bid on it, so demand the publisher already integrated goes uncollected. The check reads the page's Prebid configuration against the ads.txt file already published, so it costs review time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific line to add, correct, or remove.
intro: Prebid.js is the header-bidding library a publisher runs on their own pages, and it calls a set of demand partners on every auction. An ads.txt file at the root of the publisher's domain declares which companies are authorized to sell that inventory, and buyers read it before bidding. This subcategory checks whether the ads.txt file and the Prebid.js bidder configuration agree. The checks confirm that every demand partner the wrapper calls has a correct, current ads.txt entry, and that no stale or unauthorized lines remain that point buyers at supply paths the publisher no longer uses.

audit_checks.title: Demand partners the wrapper calls are missing correct ads.txt entries
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a bidder the Prebid.js wrapper calls has no ads.txt line, or a line with the wrong seller account id or relationship, buyers can read that supply path as unauthorized and decline to bid on it. That suppresses demand the publisher already integrated and built into the auction, so the loss reaches every impression that partner would have bid on rather than a single placement, which is why the score is high.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file is a plain-text file a publisher posts at the root of their domain (e.g., example.com/ads.txt) to declare which companies are authorized to sell their inventory. The IAB Tech Lab specification (Authorized Digital Sellers) defines the format, and each line names a seller's domain, the publisher's account id with that seller, the relationship, and an optional certification id. The relationship value is either 'DIRECT', meaning the publisher contracts with that seller directly, or 'RESELLER', meaning the publisher authorizes that company to resell the inventory on their behalf.

Prebid.js is the header-bidding library running on the publisher's pages, and it calls a list of demand partners configured in the bidder settings for each ad unit. Every one of those partners is a company selling the publisher's inventory into the open market. For a buyer to treat that supply path as legitimate, the partner needs a matching ads.txt line with the correct seller domain, the correct account id the partner assigned to the publisher, and the correct relationship. When the line is missing, or the account id does not match what the partner uses, the buyer's verification fails and the demand-side platform can decline to bid through that path.

The audit question is whether the ads.txt file accounts for every bidder the Prebid.js wrapper actually calls. The two sides drift apart over time. A publisher adds a bidder to the Prebid configuration to test new demand and forgets to add its ads.txt line. A partner changes the seller account id or the canonical domain on its ads.txt line, and the publisher's file still carries the old value. A reseller arrangement gets set up as 'DIRECT' when the partner expects 'RESELLER'. Each mismatch removes a demand path the publisher meant to keep.

A few signs point to bidders that are missing correct entries:
  • A bidder code appears in the Prebid.js bidder configuration but its seller domain has no line in ads.txt
  • An ads.txt line exists for the partner but the account id does not match the value the partner publishes in its setup guide or sellers.json record
  • A line uses 'DIRECT' or 'RESELLER' that does not match the relationship the partner expects for that account
You may want to build the list of bidders the wrapper calls, then confirm each has a correct, current ads.txt line. One approach is to read the live Prebid configuration in the browser, match each bidder against the ads.txt file, and verify the account id and relationship against the partner's own documentation.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the bidder list often grows as the revenue team tests new demand partners across sections, so the Prebid.js configuration can outrun the ads.txt file. You may want to reconcile the live bidder list against ads.txt after each demand-partner change rather than only at setup, so a newly added partner does not run for weeks without an authorizing line.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the higher-value commercial pages often carry the same wrapper and bidder list as the editorial pages, so a missing ads.txt entry suppresses demand on the inventory that clears highest. You may want to confirm the bidders calling on product and category pages all have correct ads.txt lines because the uncollected demand there costs more per impression than on lower-value pages.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid.js Bidder Params for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Build the list of demand partners the Prebid.js wrapper calls, read the live ads.txt file, and match each bidder against an entry, checking the seller domain, account id, and relationship.

browser_console.label: Browser console (Prebid debugging)
steps: Open the publisher's page, open the browser developer tools, and switch to the Console tab.
Run 'pbjs.getConfig()' to read the active Prebid.js configuration, and run 'pbjs.adUnits' or 'pbjs.getEvents()' to list the ad units and the bidder codes configured on each.
Record the full set of bidder codes the wrapper calls across the page's ad units because that is the list every ads.txt entry has to cover.
For each bidder code, look up the seller domain and the expected account id and relationship in the partner's setup guide or sellers.json record so you know what the ads.txt line should say.

ads_txt_file.label: Live ads.txt file
steps: Open the ads.txt file directly at the root of the publisher's domain (e.g., example.com/ads.txt) in the browser.
For each bidder from the Prebid configuration, search the file for a line whose first field matches the partner's seller domain.
Confirm the account id on that line matches the value the partner assigns to the publisher, and that the relationship field reads 'DIRECT' or 'RESELLER' as the partner expects.
Record any bidder with no line, a mismatched account id, or a relationship that does not match because each is a supply path a buyer can read as unauthorized.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each bidder the Prebid.js wrapper calls, whether ads.txt carries a matching line with the correct seller domain, account id, and relationship. A file where every active bidder has a correct, current entry passes. A file where one or more bidders have no line, a mismatched account id, or the wrong relationship is a fail recorded with the bidder codes and the specific field that does not match.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the bidder list from the Prebid configuration and the matching ads.txt entries above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Prebid.js wrapper on {{site_name}} calls demand partners that are missing correct ads.txt entries, so buyers can read those supply paths as unauthorized and decline to bid through them. An ads.txt line authorizes a partner to sell the inventory, and when the line is absent, or the seller account id or relationship does not match what the partner expects, the demand-side platform's verification fails and demand the publisher already integrated goes uncollected. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the live bidder list against ads.txt, adding a correct line for each partner the wrapper calls, and confirming every account id and relationship against the partner's own documentation. After the change, recheck the file against the bidder configuration so the two stay in step as partners are added or removed.

audit_checks.title: Stale or unauthorized lines remain in ads.txt
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Lines for partners the publisher no longer works with, or that were never authorized, leave supply paths declared as legitimate when they are not. That weakens the protection ads.txt is meant to give because a line for a dropped partner can let inventory be sold through a path the publisher has stopped controlling, and an unexplained line undercuts the file's value to buyers reading it for verification.

educate.base:
An ads.txt file is meant to be an exact statement of which companies are authorized to sell a publisher's inventory, so it works only when the lines in it still reflect the partners the publisher actually uses. The risk on this side of the check is not a missing line. It is a line that should no longer be there. Over time a file accumulates entries for partners that were tested and dropped, resellers from an old monetization arrangement, and lines copied in from a vendor onboarding guide that was never trimmed.

A stale line carries a real cost. While the partner's ads.txt entry stands, that company remains publicly authorized to sell the inventory even though the publisher has stopped working with it and no longer monitors what it does with that supply. The line keeps a door open that the publisher meant to close. An unauthorized line, one a publisher cannot account for at all, is a stronger signal because it can mean someone other than the publisher added it or that a partner over-claimed its relationship.

The IAB Tech Lab specification treats the file as the authoritative list, so buyers reading it for verification trust that every line is current. A file padded with entries the publisher cannot explain weakens that trust and makes the legitimate lines harder to rely on. The audit question is whether every line still maps to a partner the publisher works with today, at the correct relationship, with nothing left over from a partner that has been dropped.

A few signs point to stale or unauthorized lines:
  • An ads.txt line names a seller domain that does not appear anywhere in the live Prebid.js bidder configuration and is not a known direct or reseller arrangement outside the wrapper
  • The publisher cannot say why a given line is in the file or which arrangement it belongs to
  • Multiple lines exist for one partner with conflicting account ids or relationships, which points to an old entry that was never removed when a new one was added
You may want to read each ads.txt line against the current bidder list and the publisher's active direct and reseller arrangements, then remove any line that no longer maps to a partner in use. One approach is to treat the live Prebid configuration plus any documented off-wrapper arrangements as the source of truth and reconcile the file against it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, monetization partners change as the revenue team tests and drops demand sources, so old reseller lines tend to pile up in ads.txt. You may want to review the file whenever a partner is removed from the wrapper, so a dropped seller does not stay publicly authorized to sell the inventory after the publisher has stopped working with it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a single team often owns both the storefront and the content monetization, and lines copied from an early vendor setup can linger after the arrangement ends. You may want to confirm every ads.txt line maps to a partner the site uses today because a stale line leaves a supply path to the commercial inventory open to a company the publisher no longer controls.

learn_more.source_title: Supply Chain Object Module for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Read each line in the live ads.txt file against the current Prebid.js bidder list and any documented off-wrapper arrangements, flagging lines that no longer map to a partner in use.

ads_txt_file.label: Live ads.txt file
steps: Open the ads.txt file at the root of the publisher's domain and list every line with its seller domain, account id, and relationship.
Group lines by seller domain and note any partner that has more than one line because conflicting account ids or relationships point to an old entry left in place.
Mark each line for review against the current bidder list, separating lines you can map to an active partner from lines you cannot.

browser_console.label: Browser console (Prebid debugging)
steps: Open the publisher's page, open the browser developer tools, and run 'pbjs.getConfig()' and 'pbjs.adUnits' to read the bidder codes the wrapper actually calls.
Match each ads.txt seller domain against an active bidder code or a documented direct or reseller arrangement outside the wrapper.
Record any ads.txt line that maps to no active bidder and no documented arrangement, and confirm with the publisher whether it should stay or be removed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each ads.txt line, whether it maps to a partner the publisher uses today, either a bidder in the live Prebid configuration or a documented off-wrapper arrangement, at the correct relationship. A file where every line maps to an active, accountable partner passes. A file carrying lines for dropped partners, duplicate conflicting lines, or entries the publisher cannot explain is a fail recorded with the specific lines flagged for removal or confirmation.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each ads.txt line and whether it maps to a current partner above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ads.txt file on {{site_name}} carries stale or unexplained lines that no longer map to a partner the publisher uses. An ads.txt line keeps a company publicly authorized to sell the inventory, so a leftover entry for a dropped partner holds a supply path open after the arrangement ended, and a line the publisher cannot account for weakens the verification buyers rely on. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling each line against the live Prebid.js bidder list and any documented direct or reseller arrangements, then removing the lines that map to no current partner and resolving any duplicate or conflicting entries. After the change, set a review of the file on each demand-partner change so dropped partners do not linger as authorized sellers.
